expanded_description:
The Phillips curve represents the inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment. First documented by A.W. Phillips in 1958 using UK data (1861-1957), the curve showed that periods of low unemployment were associated with high wage inflation, and periods of high unemployment with low wage inflation. This empirical finding suggested a trade-off: policymakers could choose between lower unemployment (at the cost of higher inflation) or lower inflation (at the cost of higher unemployment). The short-run Phillips curve (SRPC) became a cornerstone of Keynesian economics and policy analysis in the 1960s.

Understanding the short-run Phillips curve is essential for analyzing the trade-offs facing central banks, evaluating the costs of disinflation, and understanding the limitations of monetary policy. The SRPC shows that in the short run, expansionary policy that reduces unemployment below the natural rate will increase inflation; contractionary policy that reduces inflation will increase unemployment above the natural rate. However, the famous Friedman-Phelps critique (1968) argued that this trade-off exists only in the short run; in the long run, the Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment. Expectations of inflation shift the SRPC: when people expect higher inflation, the curve shifts up, requiring higher unemployment to achieve the same inflation reduction.

In this section, we will derive the short-run Phillips curve from the AS-AD model, analyze the inflation-unemployment trade-off, discuss the role of expectations (adaptive vs. rational), examine the natural rate hypothesis, and explore the policy implications of the Phillips curve. You will learn to calculate the sacrifice ratio, understand why the trade-off disappears in the long run, and evaluate the historical evidence for the Phillips curve (including the 1970s breakdown).

core_concept.principle:
The short-run Phillips curve (SRPC) shows an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment. It is derived from the aggregate supply curve and can be expressed as:

Phillips Curve Equation:
$\pi = \pi^e - eta (u - u_n) + u$

where:
• $\pi$ = actual inflation rate
• $\pi^e$ = expected inflation rate
• $u$ = actual unemployment rate
• $u_n$ = natural rate of unemployment (NAIRU)
• $ eta > 0$ = sensitivity of inflation to unemployment (slope of SRPC)
• $ u$ = supply shock (e.g., oil price changes)

Key Properties:
• When $u = u_n$, actual inflation equals expected inflation ($\pi = \pi^e$).
• When $u < u_n$, inflation is higher than expected ($\pi > \pi^e$).
• When $u > u_n$, inflation is lower than expected ($\pi < \pi^e$).
• The slope $ eta$ determines how much inflation changes when unemployment deviates from the natural rate.

Derivation from Aggregate Supply:
Starting from the expectations-augmented Phillips curve:
$\pi - \pi^e = - eta (u - u_n)$

This can be rearranged to the standard form.

Natural Rate Hypothesis (Friedman, Phelps 1968):
• In the long run, the Phillips curve is vertical at $u = u_n$.
• There is no long-run trade-off between inflation and unemployment.
• The only way to keep unemployment below $u_n$ is to have ever-accelerating inflation (which is unsustainable).

Adaptive Expectations:
$\pi^e_t = \pi_{t-1}$ (simple form)

With adaptive expectations, the Phillips curve becomes:
$\pi_t - \pi_{t-1} = - eta (u_t - u_n)$

This implies that unemployment can only be kept below $u_n$ if inflation accelerates (the accelerationist hypothesis).

Sacrifice Ratio:
The cumulative output loss (or increase in unemployment) needed to reduce inflation by 1 percentage point.

$Sacrifice\ Ratio = rac{ ext{Cumulative output loss}}{ ext{Reduction in inflation}}$

Typical estimates: 2-3% of GDP per 1% disinflation.

Policy Implications:
• Central banks can use the SRPC to reduce unemployment temporarily, but at the cost of higher inflation.
• To reduce inflation permanently, the central bank must increase unemployment above $u_n$ (a recession).
• Credible monetary policy can reduce the sacrifice ratio (by reducing $\pi^e$ quickly).
• Supply shocks ($ u$) shift the SRPC, causing stagflation (high inflation + high unemployment).
core_concept.explanation:
Imagine the economy is at the natural rate of unemployment ($u_n = 5\%$) with stable inflation ($\pi = 2\%$). The central bank wants to reduce unemployment to 4% to help workers. It increases the money supply (expansionary policy). Initially, the extra spending boosts output and employment; unemployment falls to 4%. But as the economy heats up, wages and prices begin to rise. Inflation increases from 2% to 4%. In the short run, the central bank has achieved lower unemployment at the cost of higher inflation—a movement along the SRPC.

Now workers and firms adjust their expectations. They expect 4% inflation going forward. When the central bank tries to keep unemployment at 4%, it must create even higher inflation (6%) to surprise workers again. The SRPC shifts up. To keep unemployment below the natural rate, inflation must accelerate ever faster. This is the accelerationist hypothesis.

If instead the central bank decides to reduce inflation from 4% to 2%, it must create a recession: unemployment rises above the natural rate (e.g., to 7%). The sacrifice ratio measures how much unemployment (or lost output) is needed to reduce inflation by 1 percentage point. If the sacrifice ratio is 2, a 2 percentage point disinflation costs 4% of GDP.

Credible central banks can reduce the sacrifice ratio. If the central bank announces a disinflation and is believed, inflation expectations fall quickly, and the recession is milder. The Volcker disinflation (1979-1982) had a high sacrifice ratio because the Fed lacked credibility initially; by the 1990s, the Fed had credibility, and disinflations were less costly.

image.alt: Graph: Short-run Phillips curve (downward sloping) with inflation on y-axis and unemployment on x-axis. Natural rate marked where expected inflation equals actual inflation.
image.caption: Figure 10.1.1: The short-run Phillips curve shows an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment. At the natural rate of unemployment ($u_n$), actual inflation equals expected inflation.

sections.introduction:
The Phillips curve represents the inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment. First documented by A.W. Phillips in 1958 using UK data (1861-1957), the curve showed that periods of low unemployment were associated with high wage inflation, and periods of high unemployment with low wage inflation. This empirical finding suggested a trade-off: policymakers could choose between lower unemployment (at the cost of higher inflation) or lower inflation (at the cost of higher unemployment). The short-run Phillips curve (SRPC) became a cornerstone of Keynesian economics and policy analysis in the 1960s.

Understanding the short-run Phillips curve is essential for analyzing the trade-offs facing central banks, evaluating the costs of disinflation, and understanding the limitations of monetary policy. The SRPC shows that in the short run, expansionary policy that reduces unemployment below the natural rate will increase inflation; contractionary policy that reduces inflation will increase unemployment above the natural rate. However, the famous Friedman-Phelps critique (1968) argued that this trade-off exists only in the short run; in the long run, the Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment. Expectations of inflation shift the SRPC: when people expect higher inflation, the curve shifts up, requiring higher unemployment to achieve the same inflation reduction.

In this section, we will derive the short-run Phillips curve from the AS-AD model, analyze the inflation-unemployment trade-off, discuss the role of expectations (adaptive vs. rational), examine the natural rate hypothesis, and explore the policy implications of the Phillips curve.

introduction_image.alt: Scatter plot of Phillips' original data (1861-1957) showing negative relationship between wage inflation and unemployment; points clustered around a downward-sloping curve.
introduction_image.caption: Figure 10.1.2: A.W. Phillips' original data (1861-1957) showing an inverse relationship between wage inflation and unemployment in the United Kingdom.

key_concept.definition:
Short-Run Phillips Curve (SRPC): The inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment that exists in the short run due to sticky wages and prices.

Equation:
$\pi = \pi^e - eta (u - u_n) + u$

where:
• $\pi$ = actual inflation rate
• $\pi^e$ = expected inflation rate
• $u$ = actual unemployment rate
• $u_n$ = natural rate of unemployment (NAIRU)
• $ eta > 0$ = slope parameter (how much inflation responds to unemployment)
• $ u$ = supply shock (positive = bad shock, increases inflation)

Derivation from Aggregate Supply:
Starting from the expectations-augmented Phillips curve:
$\pi - \pi^e = - eta (u - u_n)$

This is the most common form. It says that unexpected inflation ($\pi - \pi^e$) is negatively related to cyclical unemployment ($u - u_n$).

Natural Rate Hypothesis (Friedman, Phelps 1968):
• In the long run, unemployment returns to the natural rate ($u_n$).
• The long-run Phillips curve is vertical at $u = u_n$.
• There is no permanent trade-off between inflation and unemployment.
• Attempts to keep unemployment below $u_n$ lead to accelerating inflation.

Adaptive Expectations:
$\pi^e_t = \pi_{t-1}$ (simplest form)

With adaptive expectations, the Phillips curve becomes:
$\pi_t - \pi_{t-1} = - eta (u_t - u_n)$

This implies that to keep unemployment below $u_n$, inflation must accelerate (the accelerationist hypothesis).

Sacrifice Ratio:
The cumulative output loss (or increase in unemployment) needed to reduce inflation by 1 percentage point.

$Sacrifice\ Ratio = rac{ ext{Cumulative output loss}}{ ext{Reduction in inflation}}$

Typical estimates: 2-3% of GDP per 1% disinflation.

Supply Shocks ($ u$):
• Positive supply shock (oil price decrease, productivity increase): $ u < 0$, shifts SRPC down (lower inflation at each unemployment rate).
• Negative supply shock (oil price increase, supply chain disruption): $ u > 0$, shifts SRPC up (higher inflation at each unemployment rate).
• Supply shocks cause stagflation (high inflation + high unemployment).
key_concept.context: The Phillips curve was the dominant framework for macroeconomic policy in the 1960s. Policymakers believed they could choose a point on the curve (e.g., 3% unemployment with 2% inflation). The 1970s stagflation (high inflation and high unemployment) discredited the simple Phillips curve. Friedman and Phelps argued that the trade-off only exists in the short run; in the long run, the Phillips curve is vertical. Their natural rate hypothesis was confirmed by the 1970s experience. Today, central banks use the expectations-augmented Phillips curve as a framework for policy analysis. The curve remains central to monetary policy, as it captures the trade-off that central banks face between stabilizing inflation and stabilizing unemployment.

image.alt: Graph: Downward-sloping short-run Phillips curves (SRPC) for different expected inflation rates, intersecting a vertical long-run Phillips curve (LRPC) at the natural rate of unemployment.
image.caption: Figure 10.1.3: The short-run Phillips curve slopes downward, but the long-run Phillips curve is vertical at the natural rate of unemployment. Expectations shifts the SRPC.

classifications.value: A.W. Phillips documented an inverse empirical relationship between wage inflation and unemployment in the UK (1861-1957)
characteristics: Wage inflation (or price inflation) falls when unemployment rises.
Unemployment falls when wage inflation rises.
Seemed to offer policymakers a menu of choices.
Used by Samuelson and Solow (1960) to estimate a US Phillips curve.
Became the foundation for Keynesian demand management policy.
Did not account for inflation expectations (was purely empirical).
classifications.behavior:
Phillips's original finding was purely empirical: he plotted wage inflation against unemployment and found a downward-sloping curve. Samuelson and Solow (1960) did the same for US price inflation and unemployment, concluding that policymakers could choose a combination (e.g., 3% unemployment with 2% inflation). This was the high watermark of Keynesian policy activism: the belief that demand management could permanently lower unemployment at the cost of modest inflation.

The original Phillips curve did not account for expectations. It worked well in the 1950s and 1960s because inflation was low and stable; expectations were anchored. But when inflation rose in the late 1960s and 1970s, the relationship broke down. The simple curve could not explain why both inflation and unemployment were high simultaneously (stagflation).

image.alt: Graph: Downward-sloping curve with data points from 1950s-1960s; 1970s data points far from the curve (stagflation).
image.caption: Figure 10.1.4: The original Phillips curve worked well in the 1950s-1960s but broke down in the 1970s when both inflation and unemployment rose (stagflation).

examples.explanation: In the 1960s, the US had unemployment around 4-5% and inflation around 1-2%. The data points clustered along a downward-sloping curve. Policymakers believed they could choose a point on the curve, e.g., targeting 3% unemployment would cause 3% inflation.

In the 1970s, the US had both high unemployment (6-9%) and high inflation (6-12%). The data points did not lie on the 1960s curve; they were far to the northeast. The simple Phillips curve failed. This led to the expectations-augmented Phillips curve.

classifications.value: The modern Phillips curve includes inflation expectations; the trade-off exists only in the short run
characteristics: When $u = u_n$, actual inflation equals expected inflation.
When $u < u_n$, actual inflation exceeds expected inflation.
When $u > u_n$, actual inflation is less than expected inflation.
In the long run, $u = u_n$ and the Phillips curve is vertical.
Expectations shift the SRPC: higher $\pi^e$ shifts the curve up.
classifications.behavior:
The expectations-augmented Phillips curve revolutionized macroeconomics. It introduced the natural rate hypothesis: there is no permanent trade-off between inflation and unemployment. If the central bank tries to keep unemployment below $u_n$, it must generate ever-higher inflation (accelerationist hypothesis). Workers and firms learn to expect higher inflation, so the SRPC shifts up, and unemployment returns to $u_n$ at a higher inflation rate.

This framework explains the 1970s stagflation: negative supply shocks ($\nu > 0$) shifted the SRPC up, causing both higher inflation and higher unemployment. It also explains the Volcker disinflation: the Fed raised unemployment above $u_n$ to reduce inflation, and eventually expectations adjusted, shifting the SRPC down.

The natural rate $u_n$ is not constant; it changes with demographics, labor market institutions, and technology. In the US, $u_n$ was about 6% in the 1970s, 5% in the 1990s, and 4% in the 2010s.

image.alt: Multiple SRPC curves shifting up as expected inflation increases; LRPC vertical at un. Arrows show movement along SRPC and shifts.
image.caption: Figure 10.1.5: The expectations-augmented Phillips curve: higher expected inflation shifts the SRPC up. In the long run, unemployment returns to the natural rate.

examples.explanation: Inflation was 9% in 1979. The Fed raised rates, increasing unemployment to 10.8% in 1982. According to the SRPC, high unemployment ($u > u_n$) would reduce inflation. Inflation fell to 3% by 1983. The sacrifice ratio was about 3-4 (high because expectations were slow to adjust).

By the 1990s, the Fed had credibility. When it raised rates to reduce inflation, expectations adjusted quickly, and the sacrifice ratio was lower (about 1-2). The 1994-1995 soft landing is an example: the Fed raised rates preemptively, and inflation remained low with only a mild recession.

classifications.value: How expectations are formed determines the dynamics of the Phillips curve and the cost of disinflation
characteristics: Adaptive expectations: $\pi^e_t = \pi_{t-1}$ (or weighted average of past inflation).
With adaptive expectations, disinflation requires a recession (high unemployment).
Rational expectations: agents use all available information, including future policy announcements.
With rational expectations, credible disinflation can be costless (no recession).
The Sargent (1982) 'rational expectations' hypothesis: if the central bank is credible, the sacrifice ratio can be zero.
Empirical evidence: disinflation usually has costs, but credibility reduces them.
classifications.behavior:
The way expectations are formed is crucial for the Phillips curve. Under adaptive expectations, people form expectations based on past inflation. If the central bank wants to reduce inflation, it must create a recession (unemployment above $u_n$) to surprise workers and firms. The sacrifice ratio is positive. This was the experience of the Volcker disinflation (1979-1982).

Under rational expectations (Sargent, Lucas), people use all available information, including the central bank's announced policy. If the central bank announces a disinflation and is believed, expectations can fall immediately without a recession. The sacrifice ratio can be zero. However, this requires credibility. If the central bank has a history of reneging on its promises (as in the 1970s), credibility is low, and disinflations are costly.

Most empirical evidence supports the middle ground: credibility reduces the sacrifice ratio but does not eliminate it entirely. The 1990s disinflations were less costly than the Volcker disinflation because central banks had built credibility. The 2021-2022 inflation surge was met with rapid Fed tightening; as of 2024, inflation has fallen from 9% to 3% with only a modest rise in unemployment (so far), suggesting a low sacrifice ratio.

image.alt: Two graphs: Adaptive expectations (disinflation requires recession) vs. Rational expectations (credible disinflation can be costless).
image.caption: Figure 10.1.6: Under adaptive expectations, disinflation requires a recession. Under rational expectations, credible disinflation can be costless (no increase in unemployment).

examples.explanation: The Fed had low credibility in 1979. Inflation expectations were adaptive (based on past inflation). The Fed had to create a deep recession (unemployment 10.8%) to reduce inflation. The sacrifice ratio was high (3-4).

New Zealand adopted an inflation-targeting framework in 1990, with a highly credible central bank. When the central bank tightened, inflation expectations fell quickly, and the disinflation was less costly than in countries without credibility.

principles.principle: The Short-Run Phillips Curve Exists Because of Sticky Wages and Prices; the Long-Run Phillips Curve Is Vertical
principles.statement: In the short run, wages and prices are sticky. When the central bank increases aggregate demand, output and employment rise, and unemployment falls below the natural rate. As the economy heats up, wages and prices begin to rise, increasing inflation. This gives the downward-sloping SRPC. However, in the long run, wages and prices adjust fully. Expectations of inflation adjust as well. The only sustainable unemployment rate is the natural rate ($u_n$). Any attempt to keep unemployment below $u_n$ leads to accelerating inflation (the accelerationist hypothesis). The long-run Phillips curve is vertical at $u_n$. This is one of the most important insights in macroeconomics: there is no permanent trade-off between inflation and unemployment. Central banks cannot target a low unemployment rate; they can only target a low and stable inflation rate. The natural rate is determined by real factors (labor market institutions, demographics, technology), not monetary policy.
principles.application: Central banks should not try to permanently lower unemployment below the natural rate. Doing so will only lead to higher inflation, not permanently lower unemployment. Instead, central banks should focus on price stability (low and stable inflation) and let the labor market determine unemployment at the natural rate. However, central banks can temporarily lower unemployment below $u_n$ during a recession (to bring the economy back to potential) and temporarily raise unemployment above $u_n$ during an overheating (to bring inflation down). The key is to recognize that the trade-off is short-run, not long-run.
principles.example: In the 1960s, the Fed tried to keep unemployment below the natural rate. Inflation rose from 1% in 1960 to 5% in 1969. The Fed then tightened, causing a recession in 1970. The attempt to permanently lower unemployment failed; unemployment returned to the natural rate, but inflation was higher. This is the accelerationist hypothesis at work.

image.alt: Graph: Movement along SRPC from point A to B (unemployment falls, inflation rises). Then SRPC shifts up (expectations adjust), and unemployment returns to un at higher inflation.
image.caption: Figure 10.1.7: The short-run Phillips curve trade-off. Attempts to keep unemployment below the natural rate lead to higher expected inflation and a shift of the SRPC, returning unemployment to the natural rate at a higher inflation rate.

principles.principle: Supply Shifts Cause Stagflation (High Inflation and High Unemployment)
principles.statement: The Phillips curve includes a supply shock term $\nu$. A negative supply shock (e.g., an oil price increase, supply chain disruption) shifts the SRPC up, causing both higher inflation and higher unemployment simultaneously. This phenomenon is called stagflation (stagnation + inflation). The 1970s oil crises are the classic example: oil prices quadrupled in 1973-1974, shifting the SRPC up, and the US experienced high inflation (11%) and high unemployment (9%). Policymakers faced a dilemma: expansionary policy would reduce unemployment but increase inflation further; contractionary policy would reduce inflation but increase unemployment further. This is why the 1970s was such a difficult period for policy. Supply shocks also affect the natural rate: a persistent negative supply shock may increase $u_n$ by reducing potential output. Understanding supply shocks is essential for analyzing policy trade-offs and evaluating central bank responses.
principles.application: When inflation is driven by supply shocks (as in 2021-2022), central banks face a more difficult trade-off. Raising interest rates to fight inflation will reduce demand and may cause a recession, but it will not directly address the supply disruption. Central banks must decide how much to react to supply-driven inflation. The Fed's 2022-2023 tightening was aggressive, contributing to a slowdown but (as of 2024) avoiding a recession. Some economists argued that the Fed should have looked through supply shocks (since they are temporary), but the persistence of the shocks (supply chains, energy, labor shortages) made that difficult.
principles.example: The 1973 oil crisis: oil prices quadrupled, shifting the SRPC up. Inflation rose from 3% to 11%, and unemployment rose from 4% to 9%. The US experienced stagflation. The Fed initially tried to accommodate (keep rates low), but inflation persisted. Volcker eventually tightened, causing a deep recession but breaking inflation.

image.alt: Graph: SRPC shifting up (negative supply shock) → both inflation and unemployment rise (stagflation).
image.caption: Figure 10.1.8: A negative supply shock shifts the short-run Phillips curve up, causing both higher inflation and higher unemployment (stagflation).

worked_examples.title: Calculating Inflation and Unemployment Using the Phillips Curve

image.caption: Figure 10.1.9: Worked example: calculating inflation for different unemployment rates using the expectations-augmented Phillips curve.

worked_examples.problem:
An economy has the following Phillips curve: $\pi = \pi^e - 0.5(u - u_n)$ where $\pi$ and $\pi^e$ are in percent. The natural rate of unemployment is $u_n = 5\%$. Expected inflation is $\pi^e = 2\%$.

a) Calculate the inflation rate when unemployment is 4%.
b) Calculate the inflation rate when unemployment is 5%.
c) Calculate the inflation rate when unemployment is 6%.
d) If the central bank wants to achieve 1% inflation, what unemployment rate must it achieve (assuming $\pi^e = 2\%$)?
e) After the central bank achieves 1% inflation, expectations adjust to $\pi^e = 1\%$. Now if unemployment returns to 5%, what is inflation?
f) What is the long-run trade-off between inflation and unemployment?

step_by_step.step: Find unemployment for target inflation of 1%
step_by_step.explanation:
Set $\pi = 1$ and solve for $u$:
$1 = 2 - 0.5(u - 5)$
$0.5(u - 5) = 2 - 1 = 1$
$u - 5 = 1 / 0.5 = 2$
$u = 5 + 2 = 7\%$

The central bank must raise unemployment to 7% to reduce inflation from 2% to 1%.

When unemployment returns to the natural rate (5%) and expectations have adjusted to $\pi^e = 1\%$, then:
$\pi = 1 - 0.5(5 - 5) = 1 - 0 = 1\%$

The economy is now at the natural rate with 1% inflation.

In the long run, unemployment returns to the natural rate ($u_n = 5\%$) regardless of inflation expectations. The long-run Phillips curve is vertical at $u_n = 5\%$. There is no long-run trade-off: any expected inflation is compatible with $u = u_n$ in the long run. The central bank can choose the inflation rate (by setting expectations) but cannot permanently change unemployment.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 2.5%. b) 2%. c) 1.5%. d) u = 7%. e) π = 1%. f) No long-run trade-off; LRPC vertical at un=5%.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the expectations-augmented Phillips curve to calculate inflation for different unemployment rates and understanding the long-run neutrality.

worked_examples.title: Calculating the Sacrifice Ratio and Disinflation Costs

image.alt: Table: Initial inflation 9%, target 2%, disinflation 7%. Sacrifice ratio 3 → cumulative output loss 21% of GDP. Okun's law: 1% unemployment → 2% output loss, so unemployment increase = (21/2)=10.5%.
image.caption: Figure 10.1.10: Worked example: calculating the output and unemployment costs of disinflation using the sacrifice ratio.

worked_examples.problem:
An economy has an initial inflation rate of 9%. The central bank wants to reduce inflation to 2% (a 7 percentage point disinflation). Historical estimates suggest the sacrifice ratio (cumulative output loss per 1 percentage point disinflation) is 2.5.

a) Calculate the cumulative output loss (as a percentage of GDP) from this disinflation.
b) If Okun's law states that a 1 percentage point increase in unemployment reduces output by 2%, what is the cumulative increase in the unemployment rate needed?
c) If the disinflation takes 2 years, what is the average annual unemployment rate increase?
d) If the central bank is highly credible and the sacrifice ratio is only 1, recalculate the output loss and unemployment increase.
e) Why might a credible central bank have a lower sacrifice ratio?

step_by_step.explanation:
Disinflation = 9% - 2% = 7 percentage points.

Sacrifice ratio = 2.5 (2.5% of GDP lost per 1% disinflation).

Cumulative output loss = 2.5 × 7 = 17.5% of GDP

Okun's law: $\Delta Y = -2 \times \Delta u$ (1% increase in unemployment reduces output by 2%).

Output loss of 17.5% implies a cumulative unemployment increase of:
$\Delta u = 17.5 / 2 = 8.75 percentage points

This means that over the disinflation period, unemployment must be, on average, 8.75 percentage points above the natural rate. For example, if the natural rate is 5%, unemployment would need to average 13.75% over the period.

step_by_step.step: Calculate average annual unemployment increase over 2 years
step_by_step.explanation:
Cumulative unemployment increase = 8.75 percentage points over 2 years.

Average annual unemployment increase = 8.75 / 2 = 4.375 percentage points above natural rate per year

So if $u_n = 5\%$, average unemployment would be 9.375% over the two years (peaking higher).

step_by_step.step: Recalculate with lower sacrifice ratio (credible central bank)
step_by_step.explanation:
If the sacrifice ratio is 1 (credible central bank):

Cumulative output loss = 1 × 7 = 7% of GDP

Cumulative unemployment increase = 7 / 2 = 3.5 percentage points

Average annual unemployment increase (over 2 years) = 3.5 / 2 = 1.75 percentage points above natural rate per year

With $u_n = 5\%$, average unemployment would be 6.75% over the two years—a much milder recession.

step_by_step.step: Explain why credibility lowers the sacrifice ratio
step_by_step.explanation:
A credible central bank can lower the sacrifice ratio because:

1. Expectations adjust faster: If people believe the central bank will achieve its inflation target, they lower their inflation expectations immediately. The Phillips curve shifts down quickly, so the same increase in unemployment produces a larger reduction in inflation.

2. Wage and price setting: When expectations are anchored, workers and firms do not build high inflation into contracts. The disinflation is less costly.

3. Historical evidence: The Volcker disinflation (1979-1982) had a high sacrifice ratio (3-4) because the Fed lacked credibility. By the 1990s, the Fed had credibility, and the 1994-1995 soft landing had a much lower sacrifice ratio (estimated 1-2).

4. Rational expectations: In the extreme (Sargent, 1982), if the central bank is fully credible, the sacrifice ratio can be zero—disinflation without a recession. This is the 'rational expectations' hypothesis. In practice, credibility reduces but does not eliminate the sacrifice ratio.

worked_examples.final_answer: a) 17.5% of GDP. b) 8.75 percentage points above natural rate. c) 4.375 percentage points per year. d) Output loss 7% of GDP, unemployment increase 3.5 percentage points. e) Credibility lowers sacrifice ratio because expectations adjust faster, shifting SRPC down more quickly.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Applying the sacrifice ratio and Okun's law to calculate the output and unemployment costs of disinflation, and understanding the role of credibility.

process_flow.process: Estimate the natural rate of unemployment (u_n)
process_flow.description: Use labor market data to estimate the NAIRU (non-accelerating inflation rate of unemployment).
process_flow.mechanism: u_n changes with demographics, labor market institutions, and technology. Common estimates: CBO's NAIRU (US).

image.alt: Diagram: Labor market data → estimate of natural rate (un).
image.caption: Figure 10.1.11: Step 1 - Estimate the natural rate of unemployment (NAIRU) from labor market data.

process_flow.process: Observe actual unemployment (u) and inflation expectations (π^e)
process_flow.description: Measure current unemployment from surveys. Measure inflation expectations from surveys (Michigan Survey, SPF) or market-based measures (TIPS breakevens).
process_flow.mechanism: u from BLS; π^e from surveys or financial markets.

image.alt: Diagram: BLS unemployment survey, inflation expectations surveys → u and π^e.
image.caption: Figure 10.1.12: Step 2 - Observe actual unemployment and expected inflation from survey and market data.

process_flow.description: Determine whether unemployment is above or below the natural rate.
process_flow.mechanism: If u < u_n → economy overheating; if u > u_n → economy in recession; if u = u_n → economy at potential.

image.alt: Equation: u - un → positive (recession) or negative (overheating).
image.caption: Figure 10.1.13: Step 3 - Calculate cyclical unemployment to determine the state of the economy.

process_flow.process: Use Phillips curve to predict inflation (π = π^e - β(u - un) + ν)
process_flow.description: Estimate inflation based on expected inflation, cyclical unemployment, and supply shocks.
process_flow.mechanism: If u < un, inflation will be higher than expected; if u > un, inflation will be lower than expected.

image.alt: Equation: π = π^e - β(u - un) + ν → predicted inflation.
image.caption: Figure 10.1.14: Step 4 - Use the Phillips curve to predict inflation based on expected inflation and cyclical unemployment.

process_flow.process: Determine policy response (target unemployment to achieve desired inflation)
process_flow.description: If inflation is too high, the central bank must raise unemployment above un (tighten policy). If inflation is too low, the central bank must lower unemployment below un (ease policy).
process_flow.mechanism: Solve for target u: u_target = un - (π_target - π^e)/β.

image.alt: Equation: u_target = un - (π_target - π^e)/β → policy action.
image.caption: Figure 10.1.15: Step 5 - Determine the target unemployment rate needed to achieve the desired inflation rate, then adjust policy accordingly.

image.alt: Master framework: Estimate un → observe u and π^e → calculate cyclical unemployment → predict inflation → determine policy response → adjust to target inflation.
image.caption: Figure 10.1.16: Complete framework for short-run Phillips curve analysis: from data to policy.

summary: The short-run Phillips curve (SRPC) shows an inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment: $\pi = \pi^e - \beta(u - u_n) + \nu$. It exists because of sticky wages and prices. When unemployment is below the natural rate ($u_n$), inflation is higher than expected; when unemployment is above $u_n$, inflation is lower than expected. The long-run Phillips curve is vertical at $u_n$: there is no permanent trade-off between inflation and unemployment. This is the natural rate hypothesis (Friedman, Phelps 1968). The SRPC shifts with expected inflation and supply shocks. Adaptive expectations ($\pi^e_t = \pi_{t-1}$) imply that keeping unemployment below $u_n$ requires accelerating inflation (accelerationist hypothesis). Rational expectations suggest that credible disinflation can be costless, but empirical evidence shows that credibility reduces but does not eliminate the sacrifice ratio. The sacrifice ratio is the cumulative output loss (or unemployment increase) needed to reduce inflation by 1 percentage point; typical estimates are 2-3% of GDP per 1% disinflation. Supply shocks ($\nu$) shift the SRPC, causing stagflation (high inflation and high unemployment), as seen in the 1970s. Understanding the short-run Phillips curve is essential for analyzing the trade-offs facing central banks, evaluating the costs of disinflation, and understanding the limitations of monetary policy in the long run.
